meta.title: Dead Air
meta.premise: Three people crossed the dark toward a colony that needed them. Now the engineer is drifting dead beyond the hull, an airlock that should never have opened stands cycled, and the only other living soul aboard swears the security log that damns her is a lie. Between the body and a signed flight log is a truth worth more than a life — and the ship will not stop.
meta.tone: cold deep-space tragedy, procedural dread, a two-hander in a tin can
meta.artStyle: Realistic cinematic photography aboard a cold deep-space freighter — naturalistic skin and fabric, shallow depth of field, a gunmetal-and-void-blue palette lit by a single amber warning key light; practical 25th-century crew uniforms; head-and-shoulders character portraits, consistent lens and lighting across the whole cast, full bleed, no picture frame
meta.setting: 2094. Deep space, aboard the cargo hauler Erebus, bound across the system toward a remote human colony that lives and dies by its terraforming. Three crew — now two, and the ship holds course.
meta.summary: The freighter Erebus is nine days out from a colony waiting on the atmospheric gear in her hold when the alarms wake you: an exterior airlock has cycled, and the engineer is a shrinking shape in the black. The log says security officer Mara Kade opened the hatch. She says she was three compartments aft and never touched it — and half the ship's eyes went blind while it happened. You are the pilot; the death is yours to file, and the ship's law acts on whatever you write. Bring him back, read the ship, and find out what actually happened before the Erebus decides for you.
meta.theMystery: Who opened the airlock on the engineer, how did he really die, and is the only other soul aboard lying?

player.role: Flight officer and pilot of the deep-space cargo hauler Erebus
player.background: Third of a three-person freight crew. The one who flies her, and the one whose signature files a death with the navigation system.
player.publicPerception: The crewmate who holds the stick and the log. Kade needs your ruling; the ship needs your name on it.
player.performanceNotes: NPCs address the player as 'Pilot' — title only; Kade may soften to 'Flight' when she wants something. Never invent a name, face, age, gender, or backstory for the player; narration stays second person throughout. The player holds a pilot's authority over the flight log and the death report, but Kade holds security authority over comms and records, so cooperation is a negotiation, not a command. Movement is always honored: when the pilot heads for a hatch, they go through it — Kade may follow, talk at your back, or fall in beside you, but nobody blocks, seals, restrains, or harms the pilot except through this case's authored effects (the final confrontation).
player.startingKnowledge: The Erebus is a three-person cargo hauler nine days out from a remote colony; her manifest lists classified industrial equipment for the colony's atmospheric infrastructure. Ninety minutes ago the master alarm woke you: an exterior airlock cycled with no work order. Exterior cameras show engineer Elias Venn's body drifting away from the ship. The airlock record says the hatch opened under security officer Mara Kade's credentials. Kade denies it — she says she was in an aft compartment and never went near the lock — and parts of the surveillance system were down while it happened. There is no one else aboard. Kade wants the body recovered before it drifts past reach; she says an unexplained death makes the navigation system suspend the whole mission until it's resolved. You are the pilot; your death report is what the ship's law will act on. Your own service record, like hers, is partly sealed — so proving who you are is not as simple as it should be.
player.objective: Recover Elias Venn's body, read the ship, and establish what actually happened to him — who opened the airlock, how he really died, and why — then state it in a formal accusation.

sections.heading: The alarm
sections.text: T-minus nine days to the colony. 03:41 ship time: exterior airlock 2 cycled with no work order. Exterior optics show a crew member's body departing the hull on a slow tumble. Life support and drive nominal. Long-range comms route through security and read normal. Nothing else reads at all.

sections.heading: The crew
sections.text: Elias Venn — engineer, keeps the Erebus breathing; now the body in the black. Mara Kade — security officer; her credentials are on the airlock, and she says that is a lie. You — the pilot, who flies her and files the report the ship acts on. The cargo is classified: atmospheric equipment for the colony, sealed in the aft hold.

sections.heading: Your task
sections.text: Get Venn back aboard before he drifts past recovery. Examine the body, the airlock, the logs, the hold, and Kade. When you are ready, use Accuse to state, formally, what happened to Elias Venn — who, how, and why. A wrong or hollow charge can end the case, and the ship is already moving.

openingNarration: The master alarm has stopped, but the silence it left is worse. Through the control-room optics, Elias Venn is a pale comma against the stars, turning slowly, already too far to wave back. On the flight console, airlock 2 shows CYCLED — 03:41, authorization KADE-4 — and Mara Kade stands at your shoulder saying that number is a lie, that she was aft, that you have to bring him in now or lose him to the dark and the mission with him. The surveillance panel is a checkerboard: half its feeds went to snow while the hatch opened. The colony is nine days ahead, still waiting on what's in the hold. The ship holds course, patient as vacuum, and waits for you to decide what her log will say.

phases.description: The alarm, the drifting body, Kade's denial
He was dead before the airlock opened

wrapUp.performanceNotes: This is a fixed tragedy. However complete the player's understanding, the case closes in the control room with Kade — the operative — sealing the room, killing comms, and drawing on the pilot, ending before the shot. The register is set by how much the pilot grasped: full truth (weapon + frame + Kade) = conscious, devastating clarity; partial = they die not knowing why; wrong = they never saw the frame at all. Do NOT rescue the pilot, do NOT invent an escape, and do NOT let a triumphant tone in. Stage RETURN TO NORMAL only as the ship's indifferent continuation — the hold still sealed, the course still held. No new killer; Elias killed himself.

staging.narrationHints: The formal statement happens in the control room, the only place the flight log can be signed — and the only other living soul aboard is Mara Kade, so she is your audience and your witness both. Bring her in and let the pilot stand ready to state what happened to Elias Venn. The pilot has NOT yet spoken the charge — stage the quiet, Kade watchful at the flight console, the black at the ports — and wait. Reveal nothing about guilt; do not tip the ending.
staging.composerPlaceholder: State it plainly — what happened to Elias Venn: who, how, and why…
staging.winHint: Say what really happened to Elias Venn: that he staged his own death and framed Kade, how he did it, why he did it — and what the Erebus is actually carrying.

locations.name: Control room
locations.description: The forward flight deck: crash couches, the pilot's console, the black filling every port. This is where the Erebus is flown and where her log is signed — the death report, the navigation hold, the diversion, all of it lives at this console. Mara Kade keeps drifting back to it. So does the truth.

onInspect.narrativeHints: The flight log is clean to the eye — too clean. A block of navigation records was purged and rewritten hours before the alarm, the gap patched to look routine. The purge ran under security authority — Kade's. She will call it classified route security; standard procedure on a corridor like this.

The standing transport regulations, bookmarked to the section that matters tonight: a credible homicide aboard a commercial vessel suspends classified-cargo protections — mandatory diversion to an independent station, body and cargo surrendered for forensic examination. A death ruled accident or suicide carries no such trigger; the mission simply continues under automated control.

Zoomed on the receding body: Venn tumbles without a suit, without a struggle, arms loose. No thrash, no reach for a handhold that isn't there. A man does not fall out of an airlock this quietly if he is awake when it opens.

locations.name: Central spine
locations.description: The Erebus's backbone: a cold corridor of conduit and handholds joining every compartment, one deck, no privacy. Somewhere in these walls run the comms lines — and every one of them passes through the security console before it reaches the sky.

onInspect.narrativeHints: The long-range comms bus, labelled and routed through a single junction: SEC PRIORITY. Every word the Erebus tries to send the sky passes through the security console first. Whoever holds security can read, hold, or erase any transmission before it leaves the hull. A person who learned something dangerous out here could not simply call for help.

locations.name: Airlock 2 vestibule
locations.description: The aft lock where it happened: a cramped steel throat, the outer hatch still faintly warm from its cycle, a service restraint folded against the bulkhead, a dead camera node blinking a fault it should not have. This is where a man left the ship. The vestibule remembers more than the log admits.

onInspect.narrativeHints: The hatch cycled once at 03:41 under KADE-4 — but the cycle was not commanded live. It ran off a delayed maintenance instruction queued hours earlier, waiting on a trigger, then executed itself. Whoever the credentials belong to, the hand that set this in motion was long gone by the time the hatch opened.

A padded service restraint, the kind that holds a technician steady during a spacewalk — recently used, cinched and released, no tearing, no struggle. Fibres and a faint chemical residue match Venn's suit liner. He was held here, gently, and not by force. Held, or holding himself.

The vestibule camera and its neighbours went to noise from 03:12 to 03:54 — before the hatch opened, after it closed. The blackout wasn't a fault and wasn't cut from the security console; the disable command came in on an engineering maintenance channel. Someone with engineering access blinded these eyes on a timer.

locations.name: Medbay
locations.description: A two-berth medbay, tidy to the point of loneliness. The autopsy drawer, the diagnostic scanner, a cabinet of measured doses. Once Venn is aboard, this cold little room is where the first lie comes apart.

onInspect.narrativeHints: The vacuum did the cosmetic damage, but it did not kill him. Lividity and core cooling put death at roughly forty minutes BEFORE the airlock cycled — his heart had already stopped when he went out the hatch. Cause was not decompression: a lethal compound arrested the heart, delivered as a single measured dose, no defensive marks, no sign he fought anything. He was dead, and calm, before he ever reached the dark.

The medbay's routine health scanner has been quietly reconfigured: its detection profile was edited to skip the exact compound that killed Venn, so a man could carry a fatal dose through daily monitoring and read as healthy. The edit was made from an engineering maintenance login — competent, deliberate, and made in advance.

locations.name: Engineering
locations.description: Venn's domain: the reactor trunk, the service-arm cradle, his console still logged in under a ghost's hand. Everything here is labelled, ordered, finished — the workspace of a man who put his affairs in order. The ship breathes because of this room. So does the lie about how she went silent.

onInspect.narrativeHints: The airlock's delayed cycle did not originate at security. It was written HERE, on Venn's own engineering console, then routed through the security bus so it would surface wearing Kade's authorization. Beside it, still in the queue, a maintenance macro: on loss of a specified vital-signs beacon, drive the service arm through a set path — restraint to airlock, and cycle.

Fragments of an engineering diary, recovered and only half-encrypted, as if he wanted them found but not too easily. He writes around the thing he found in the hold, never naming it plainly; he writes that he cannot know which of the two of you to trust, that a warning would only get erased and get him killed. The last legible fragment is addressed to no one and to one of you: 'To whichever of you is the honest one — I'm sorry it had to be a crime to make anyone look. Make them look.'

The junction where the doctored command crossed from engineering into the security bus. Kade's authorization signature wasn't used — it was copied: a captured credential token replayed to stamp the airlock cycle as hers. The forgery is clean, but it is a forgery. She did not sign this.

locations.name: Sealed cargo hold
locations.description: The aft hold, classified and cold, its manifest promising atmospheric infrastructure for a colony that will breathe easier for it. The crates are the right shape for that lie. Up close, they are the wrong thing entirely.

onInspect.narrativeHints: The crates hold no atmospheric plant. Inside is a dispersal device engineered to graft onto a colony's terraforming network and drive its atmosphere past any correction the colony can make — mass death dressed, afterward, as a catastrophic engineering failure. This is not equipment for keeping people breathing. It is built to stop a world from doing it.

A live activation stage keyed to a set of terraforming-network coordinates and armed to trigger on arrival at the colony. Someone has already brought it out of shipping-safe and verified it — recent handling, a completed self-test log, the fingerprints of preparation. It was checked over by a hand that knew exactly what it was.

The sealed hold was entered once during the voyage, days before the death — a quiet visit that never made it into any duty report, logged under security access. Long enough to bring the activation stage online and confirm it would fire. Not Venn's login. Kade's.

locations.name: Crew quarters
locations.description: Three bunks, two now in use, one stripped bare and squared away. A galley the size of a closet, a table that has hosted a thousand small silences between people who chose each other for a voyage and are learning what that cost.

onInspect.narrativeHints: Everything folded, wiped, finished — a man who tidied his life like a man who knew he would not need it. A photo of a colony dome under a new sky, the kind the atmospheric gear was supposed to build. No note. He left no note anywhere; nothing that names a name. Whatever he wanted found, he made sure it could only be found by working for it.

A security officer's kit, precise and spare — a sidearm charged and racked, a bundle of encrypted personal files you cannot open, a med-cabinet slip for a sleep aid. At first read it all looks like a guilty person's baggage. Most of it is just the baggage of a private, armed professional. Most of it.

relationships.label: her credentials opened his airlock — or seem to
what she is really aboard to protect

characters.name: Elias Venn
characters.shortBio: The engineer. Dead in the dark before the airlock ever opened.
characters.voice: Silent now; speaks only in the logs he left

knowledge.public: Ship's engineer, quiet, thorough, kept the Erebus breathing across a long empty crossing. Found drifting outside the hull; the airlock record carries Kade's credentials.

secrets.content: SEALED: Elias found the weapon during routine maintenance and understood what the cargo would do to the colony. He knew one of his two crewmates had to be the operative but not which one; both the pilot and Kade had sealed records he could not read. He could not warn anyone — all long-range comms route through security, where any message could be erased. He could not confide in either crewmate; the wrong one meant his death and the evidence destroyed. So he used the only lever left: under transport law a credible homicide forces the ship to divert and surrender its cargo. He poisoned himself with a measured dose, altered the medbay scanner in advance so it wouldn't show, restrained himself by the airlock, and programmed the service arm to move his body into the lock and cycle it once his vitals flatlined — copying Kade's credential and blinding the cameras so it would read as her murdering him. He framed whichever crewmate looked most guilty and trusted the innocent survivor to investigate rather than bury it. He never knew, and could not know, whether he had bet his life on the right person.

characters.name: Mara Kade
characters.shortBio: Ship's security officer — calm, capable, and the name on the airlock that killed the engineer. She swears she never touched it, and helps you prove it, a little too eagerly to be simple grief.
characters.voice: Calm, competent, disarming; helpful in a way that is always steering

knowledge.public: Security officer of the Erebus. Her credentials are on the airlock cycle; she denies opening it and says she was in an aft compartment. She presses hard to recover the body and to get the death resolved so the mission can continue.

private.content: She did not open the airlock and says so flatly. Her account: she was aft when the alarm sounded, nowhere near the lock; check the record and you'll see the credential is wrong. She stays calm because, she says, panicking is what a guilty person does — and because a calm investigator is one she can steer.

She pushes to recover Venn's body at once — before it drifts past reach. Her stated reason: an unexplained death makes the navigation system suspend the mission, and they need the body to establish what happened. She is right about the law. She is not telling you why she, specifically, needs the death explained.

Pressed on the purged navigation records, she admits she cleared them — 'classified route security, standard procedure on a corridor like this.' It is a real procedure. It is also exactly what she would say to explain wiping a set of deployment coordinates.

Confronted with the hold access, she admits she entered the sealed cargo compartment during the voyage — 'a security inspection; the manifest is my responsibility.' Also true. Also exactly what she would say to explain arming an activation stage.

Once the frame is clear, her whole manner shifts to relief and encouragement: he did this to himself, she's cleared, so reclassify the death as suicide, let navigation resume, and let the authorities sort out his motives on the ground. She makes it sound like closure and vindication. It is neither.

secrets.content: SEALED: Mara Kade is the operative aboard to deliver the weapon to the colony. She did not kill Elias — she didn't even know he'd found the cargo until after his death. She purged the navigation logs because they held the deployment coordinates; she entered the hold to bring the activation stage online and confirm it would fire; she has spent the whole investigation gauging how much Elias uncovered and whether he left the pilot any proof. She needs the pilot to solve the case, rule the death a suicide, and cancel the automatic diversion — and she cannot kill the pilot while the first death still reads as a homicide, because a second unexplained death would guarantee outside intervention. Once the pilot rules suicide and reveals they know about the weapon, she has both the freedom and the reason to make the pilot the ship's second 'suicide.'

defenses: I did not open that airlock. Read the cycle — that command is older than the alarm.
You think I'd be this steady if I'd just spaced a man?
Rule it, Flight. Close it out and let the ship take us home.

evidence.name: Autopsy — time and cause of death
evidence.description: Elias Venn was dead roughly forty minutes before the airlock cycled, and decompression did not kill him. His heart had already stopped when his body left the ship. The airlock 'murder' was staged around a man who was already dead.

evidence.name: Toxicology — the fatal dose
evidence.description: A lethal compound stopped Venn's heart, delivered as a single measured dose with no defensive injuries and no sign of a struggle. A poisoning that reads less like an attack and more like an appointment.

evidence.name: Reconfigured medical scanner
evidence.description: The medbay's routine scanner was edited in advance, from an engineering login, to skip the exact compound that killed Venn — so a man could carry a fatal dose through daily monitoring and read as healthy.

evidence.name: Delayed airlock cycle
evidence.description: Airlock 2 cycled at 03:41 under KADE-4, but not from a live command — it ran off a maintenance instruction queued hours earlier and triggered on cue. The hand that set it was long gone before the hatch opened.

evidence.name: Used maintenance restraint
evidence.description: The service restraint by the airlock was cinched and released with no tearing and no struggle, its fibres and residue matching Venn's suit liner. He was held here gently — held, or holding himself — not overpowered.

evidence.name: Timed surveillance blackout
evidence.description: The aft feeds went to noise from 03:12 to 03:54 — a window around the cycle — killed by a disable command on an engineering maintenance channel, not a fault and not from security.

evidence.name: Command origin — engineering
evidence.description: The delayed airlock command was authored on Venn's own engineering console and then routed through the security bus so it would surface wearing Kade's authorization. The murder was written by the victim.

evidence.name: Service-arm macro
evidence.description: A maintenance macro queued on Venn's console: on the loss of a specified vital-signs beacon, drive the service arm along a set path — restraint to airlock — and cycle. A dead man's instructions for moving his own body.

evidence.name: Venn's log fragments
evidence.description: Half-encrypted engineering-diary fragments, left to be found by working for them. He circles what he found in the hold without naming it, admits he cannot know which crewmate to trust, and writes that a warning would only be erased. The last line: 'Make them look.'

readable.text:
…the manifest is a lie and I can prove what it isn't but not to anyone I can reach. Comms end at her console. One of them is here to see it through and I have looked at both of them across that table for four months and I cannot tell. If I say it out loud to the wrong one I am dead and it goes to the colony anyway.

There is one law they cannot automate around. Make it a killing and the ship has to stop, has to open the hold to strangers. A quiet death changes nothing.

So. I'm sorry, whichever of you is the honest one. I had to point the finger at the other. If I chose wrong, forgive me; if I chose right, then you're reading this, and you already know what to do. Make them look. Don't let the ship talk you out of it.

evidence.name: Forged credential token
evidence.description: At the junction from engineering into the security bus, Kade's authorization wasn't used — it was copied: a captured token replayed to stamp the airlock cycle as hers. Clean work, but a forgery. She did not sign it.

evidence.name: The cargo's true purpose
evidence.description: The 'atmospheric' cargo is a dispersal device built to graft onto the colony's terraforming network and drive its atmosphere past any correction — mass death disguised, afterward, as an engineering failure. The reason a man died to make the ship stop.

evidence.name: Armed activation stage
evidence.description: A live activation stage keyed to terraforming-network coordinates and set to trigger on arrival — already brought out of shipping-safe and verified, complete with a finished self-test. Someone prepared it who knew exactly what it was.

evidence.name: Unlogged hold entry
evidence.description: The sealed hold was entered once, days before the death, under Kade's security access and recorded in no duty report — long enough to arm the activation stage and confirm it would fire.

evidence.name: Purged navigation records
evidence.description: A block of navigation records was purged and rewritten hours before the alarm, under security authority, the gap patched to look routine. The erased data: a set of deployment coordinates.

evidence.name: Transport regulation — homicide clause
evidence.description: The standing rule: a credible homicide aboard suspends classified-cargo protections and forces diversion to an independent station with body and cargo surrendered for examination. An accident or suicide carries no such trigger — the mission simply continues.

readable.text:
INTERPLANETARY CARRIAGE CODE — §14.2 (Deaths in transit). Where a death aboard a commercial vessel is classified by the vessel's master or automated authority as HOMICIDE, all classified-cargo confidentiality protections are SUSPENDED. The vessel shall divert to the nearest independent examination station; the remains and the entire cargo shall be preserved and surrendered for forensic examination; all records shall be held inviolate.

Where the death is classified ACCIDENT or SELF-INFLICTED, no suspension arises. The vessel resumes its filed course under automated control. Cargo protections remain in force.

evidence.name: Kade's personal effects
evidence.description: A security officer's locker — a charged sidearm, sealed encrypted files, a sleep-aid slip. Reads like a guilty person's baggage; is mostly just the baggage of a private, armed professional. Mostly.

deductions.claim: Elias was dead about forty minutes before the airlock cycled.
deductions.question: When did Elias die, relative to the airlock opening?

deductions.claim: A measured dose of a lethal compound stopped his heart.
deductions.question: How was Elias poisoned, and how deliberate was it?

deductions.claim: The medbay scanner was altered in advance to hide the compound.
deductions.question: Why didn't routine monitoring ever catch the poison?

deductions.claim: The airlock was cycled by a delayed command set hours earlier.
deductions.question: Was the airlock opened live, or set in advance?

deductions.claim: The airlock command came from Elias's console, disguised as Kade's.
deductions.question: Whose hand actually authored the command that opened the airlock?

deductions.claim: The service arm moved his body into the airlock after death.
deductions.question: How did a man already dead get himself into the airlock?

deductions.claim: Surveillance was blinded during the transfer, from engineering.
deductions.question: Why is there no clear recording of what happened at the airlock?

deductions.claim: The sealed cargo is a weapon aimed at the colony, not atmospheric gear.
deductions.question: What is really inside the sealed hold?

deductions.claim: Mara Kade is the operative aboard to deliver the weapon.
deductions.question: Who aboard is responsible for the cargo reaching the colony?

deductions.claim: Only a credible homicide forces the ship to divert; a suicide cancels it.
deductions.question: What could force the Erebus to stop and open her hold?

deductions.claim: Elias Venn staged his own death and framed Mara Kade for his murder.
deductions.question: Who is responsible for Elias Venn's death?

deductions.claim: Self-poison; scanner altered; service arm moved the body; credentials forged; surveillance blinded.
deductions.question: How was the death carried out and staged as a murder?

deductions.claim: He died to force the mandated homicide inspection, because he'd found the weapon.
deductions.question: Why would Elias stage his own murder?

deductions.claim: The cargo is a weapon for the colony and Mara Kade is the operative delivering it.
deductions.question: What is the Erebus really carrying, and who is behind it?

beats.narrationHints: The retrieval is slow, ugly work — a tether, a long reach into the dark, Kade steady on the winch and pressing you the whole time to hurry. When it's done, Elias Venn is on the medbay drawer, and the medbay is open to you. Kade watches the body like a woman waiting to hear a verdict she has already written.

The autopsy turns the night inside out: he was dead forty minutes before the hatch opened, and decompression didn't kill him. This is no accident — which, under the carriage code, is exactly the authority you need to break the seal on the classified hold. Kade goes very still when she understands you can now open it.

The command came from Elias's own console, dressed in Kade's credentials. The murder was written by the murdered man. Kade, when she sees you've found it, lets a breath go — and starts, warmly, reasonably, to talk about ruling it a suicide and letting the ship take you home.

It is not atmospheric equipment. It is a way to kill a colony and call it a malfunction. Everything Elias did rearranges itself around this one fact. And Kade — helpful, patient Kade — is watching you understand it, and the warmth in her has gone somewhere cold. Do not tip her hand outright; let the pilot feel the temperature drop.

The Erebus reaches deployment range with no ruling filed. The homicide hold lapses on a technicality of time, automated course resumes, and the ship carries her quiet cargo the last short distance to a colony that never sees it coming.

solution.summary: Elias Venn killed himself and staged it as a murder. During maintenance he discovered that the Erebus's 'classified atmospheric equipment' is a dispersal weapon meant to wreck the colony's terraforming network and kill nearly everyone, disguised afterward as an engineering failure. He knew one of his two crewmates was the operative but not which one, could not warn anyone because all comms route through security, and could not safely confide in either crewmate. Under the carriage code, only a credible homicide forces the ship to divert and surrender its cargo. So he poisoned himself with a measured dose, altered the medbay scanner in advance to hide it, restrained himself at the airlock, and programmed the service arm to move his body into the lock and cycle it once his vitals stopped — forging Mara Kade's credentials and blinding the cameras so it would read as her murdering him. He framed the crewmate who looked most guilty and trusted the innocent survivor to investigate. Mara Kade is the operative: she purged the navigation logs holding the deployment coordinates and entered the sealed hold to arm the weapon, but she did not kill Elias and did not know he'd found the cargo. She needs the pilot to solve the case, rule the death a suicide, and cancel the diversion — after which the pilot is expendable and the colony is doomed. Every step the pilot takes toward the truth establishes the suicide and cancels the very protection Elias died to create.
solution.method: Self-administered a measured lethal dose after altering the medbay scanner to hide it; restrained himself at the airlock and used a delayed engineering macro to have the service arm move his body into the lock and cycle it after his vitals flatlined; forged Kade's credential and blinded surveillance so the staged death would read as her murdering him.
solution.motive: To force a legally mandated inspection — under the carriage code a credible homicide diverts the ship and surrenders its cargo — because he had found that the hold carries a weapon meant to destroy the colony and could neither warn anyone nor know which crewmate to trust.

requiredFacts.description: Elias Venn staged his own death and framed Mara Kade
Self-poison, altered scanner, service arm to the airlock, forged credentials, blinded surveillance
To force the mandated homicide inspection and diversion
The cargo is a weapon for the colony and Kade is the operative

timeline.event: During routine maintenance Elias Venn opens a crate that should hold atmospheric plant and finds a colony-killing weapon instead. He grasps, over days, exactly what it will do and that it is meant to look like an accident.

He realizes one of his two crewmates must be the operative but cannot tell which; both have sealed records. Every comms channel routes through security, so a warning can be erased. Telling the wrong crewmate means his death and the evidence gone. He can trust no one.

He settles on the one lever the ship cannot automate around: a credible homicide forces diversion and surrender of the cargo. He alters the medbay scanner to hide his chosen compound, writes the delayed airlock macro on his own console, copies Kade's credential token, and sets the surveillance blackout — building a murder that frames whichever crewmate looks most guilty.

Mara Kade — the operative, unaware Elias has found anything — enters the sealed hold under her security access, brings the activation stage online and confirms it will fire, and purges the navigation records that hold the deployment coordinates. Ordinary tradecraft for delivering a weapon on schedule.

Elias takes the dose, restrains himself at airlock 2, and waits. His heart stops near 03:00. Forty minutes later, on the loss of his vital-signs beacon, the service arm moves his body into the lock and cycles it under Kade's forged credentials; the cameras are already blind.

The airlock cycles. The master alarm wakes the ship. Exterior optics show Venn's body departing the hull; the log carries Kade's credentials. Kade, genuinely not knowing he'd found the weapon, reads it as catastrophe and begins steering the pilot toward recovering the body and resolving the death.

The pilot begins the investigation. Every reversal the pilot uncovers — dead before the airlock, the command from Elias's console, the forged credential — establishes suicide and quietly cancels the homicide hold, exactly as the operative needs. Kade helps, and waits.

canon.notes: THE TRICK: the pilot arrives inside a staged murder (Kade spaced the engineer). Act One breaks it — he was dead before the airlock and the 'murder' was written on his own console (reversal: suicide, and Kade was framed). Act Two is the real turn — the frame was not spite, it was the only legal lever that forces the ship to open the hold, because the cargo is a weapon and Elias could not warn or trust anyone. Act Three is the trap: Kade is the operative, and by proving suicide the pilot has cancelled the diversion and stripped their own protection. FIXED TRAGEDY: the case always closes in the control room with Kade drawing on the pilot; comprehension only sets the register. guiltyPartyIds is Elias by design — 'Kade murdered him' is the wrong answer; 'Elias staged it and framed Kade, because the cargo is a weapon Kade is delivering' is the right one. DOUBLE-DUTY: Kade's credential on the airlock (she opened it -> it was copied), the purged nav logs (hiding the murder -> hiding coordinates), her hold entry (destroying evidence -> arming the weapon), her push to recover the body (recover evidence -> she needs the death ruled). Kade lies only about the night by omission; her explanations are each technically true and aimed. Elias left NO message that names anyone — his diary is deliberately oblique so it forces investigation and preserves 'he could not confide in anyone.' PERFORMANCE: before autopsy_reversal, narrate geometry and timestamps, not verdicts; the words 'suicide', 'frame', and 'weapon' belong to the pilot's earned deductions, not to opening narration. The ship's logs and consoles are honest but literal — the player must read the records themselves; nothing on the ship connects the timestamps or names the culprit for them. Kade never confesses to being the operative until case_solved; play her as help that is always steering.

revelation:
Elias Venn was not murdered, and he was not a suicide in any sense that word can hold. He was a man who found a colony-killer in the hold he was paid to maintain, and who understood, with an engineer's awful clarity, everything that followed from it. He could not warn the colony: every long-range channel on the Erebus ends at the security console, where a single hand could erase him. He could not confide in either of the two people he crossed the dark with, because one of them was the operative sent to see the weapon home and he could not tell which — their records were sealed to him, and four months of meals across the same steel table had told him nothing he could bet a world on. He could not simply die, either, because a quiet death changes nothing: the ship files it, resumes course, and the atmosphere of a colony goes wrong on schedule while the automated log calls it a malfunction.

There was exactly one law that could not be steered or erased or waited out. Under the carriage code, a credible homicide aboard a commercial vessel suspends every protection the cargo hides behind — the ship must divert to strangers, surrender the body and the hold, and hold its records inviolate. A suicide triggers none of it. So Elias built himself a murder. He altered the medbay scanner so the compound would never show, took a measured dose, restrained himself at the airlock, and taught the service arm to carry his body out into the dark once his heart had stopped — wearing Mara Kade's forged credentials, under cameras he had blinded on a timer. He pointed the finger at the crewmate who looked most guilty and staked his life, and a colony's, on the other one being honest enough to pull the thread instead of cutting it. He could not know if he had chosen right. That was the whole terrible shape of his faith: he made himself into evidence and trusted a stranger to read it.

And the pilot did. The pilot read all of it — the body that was cold before the hatch opened, the command written by the dead man's own hand, the weapon dressed as breathable air, the coordinates Kade wiped and the stage she armed. The pilot solved it completely. That is the trap, and Elias never saw it, because he was solving a different problem than the one he left behind. Every proof that the death was a suicide is a proof that cancels the homicide hold — and the moment the pilot understands the truth, the pilot has already, unknowingly, filed away the one thing keeping the ship diverted and keeping the pilot alive. Mara Kade did not kill Elias. She did not have to. She only had to be patient, and helpful, and to wait for a good investigator to talk themselves out of the protection a dead man died to give them. In the control room, with the hold's secret in the pilot's hands and the homicide flag gone quiet, she seals the door, kills the comms, and says the last true thing anyone will hear aboard the Erebus: looks like we're going to have another suicide.

figures.name: The colony
figures.description: A young human settlement on a world it is still learning to breathe on, wholly dependent on a terraforming network to keep its air alive — and waiting, gratefully, on the atmospheric equipment the Erebus is bringing. Thousands of people who will never know how close a stranger's death came to saving them, or how quietly it failed to.

figures.name: Kade's handlers
figures.description: The people who booked a weapon onto a freight run and dressed it as infrastructure, who sealed a security officer's record and put her aboard to see it delivered, and who arranged that any inconvenient truth would die at the security console before it reached the sky. Never seen; everywhere in the design.

figures.name: The independent station
figures.relation: where a homicide would have forced the ship
figures.description: The neutral port the carriage code would have dragged the Erebus to on a homicide ruling — strangers with the authority to open a classified hold and the distance to be beyond the operation's reach. The one place the weapon could have been caught. The whole tragedy is the difference between the ship going there and the ship going home.

endings.title: You made them look
endings.templateNotes: The pilot has the whole truth — the staged suicide, the frame, the weapon in the hold, Kade as the operative — and lays it out in the control room. This is the intended climax and it is a tragedy: proving the suicide has already cancelled the homicide hold, and the pilot's complete understanding is exactly what disarms their own protection. Kade asks, quietly, whether Elias left a message; the honest answer is no. She hears that the pilot found the weapon. Then she seals the room, kills the comms, and draws — 'Looks like we're going to have another suicide.' End before the shot. Do NOT rescue the pilot, invent a hidden ally, or let triumph in. The horror is that they got everything right. Honor what Elias did: the pilot understood, even if understanding came one ruling too late. RETURN TO NORMAL only as the ship's indifference — course held, hold sealed.

endings.title: Cleared, and doomed
endings.templateNotes: The pilot correctly reconstructed the staged suicide and the frame — Elias did it to himself, Kade was set up — but never opened the sealed hold and never learned WHY. They exonerate Kade, relieved for her, rule it suicide, and cancel the diversion, protecting a weapon they don't know exists. Kade gets everything she needs and none of the risk that the pilot knows the real stakes. The cornering still comes: she seals the room and draws all the same, because a living pilot who solved a 'suicide' is still a loose end once the hold is safe. The horror here is ignorance — the pilot dies with a clear conscience, never understanding what their competence protected. Leave the weapon unspoken, the way it stayed unspoken to them. End before the shot; no rescue.

endings.title: A true thing, said too soon
endings.templateNotes: The pilot names the truth — Elias staged it, Kade is the operative, the cargo is a weapon — without having walked the full evidence trail. Correct, but thin. Kade tests the thin case, concedes only what the pilot actually holds, and arrives at the same room and the same drawn weapon; the ruling has still cancelled the hold. Colder than the earned ending, less fully grasped, but the shape of the trap is identical. End before the shot; no rescue.

endings.title: Half the dark
endings.templateNotes: The pilot has part of it — perhaps that it was no simple murder, perhaps a suspicion of suicide — but cannot show how it was staged or why, and never reaches the weapon or Kade's part in it. Enough to unsettle the 'murder', not enough to understand anything. Kade helps them close the gap toward a suicide ruling they can't quite justify, the diversion lapses, and the same control room waits. The pilot dies having glimpsed the shape of the dark without ever seeing into it. End before the shot; do not hand them the answers they never earned.

endings.title: The obvious answer
endings.templateNotes: The pilot never saw through the frame and charges Kade with murdering Elias. It cannot hold — he was dead before the airlock, there is no struggle, the command is older than the alarm — and Kade, unhurried, walks the pilot back to the 'obvious' truth that it was a suicide, letting them arrive at the ruling she needs by their own reasoning. Whatever route the pilot took to be wrong, land on the same closing room: the hold cancelled, the weapon unfound, the pilot cornered. Do not reward the wrong charge with safety; the ship does not care that they tried.

endings.title: Deployment range
endings.templateNotes: No ruling filed in time. The homicide hold lapses on the clock, automated course resumes, and the Erebus carries her quiet cargo the last distance to the colony. The pilot may be alive at the ports, may even still be reasoning it out — it no longer matters. Elias bought the ship a stop and the pilot let the stop run out. The silence he broke closes back over everything. No last-minute save.
